summary: Thai Lee shares her personal journey from an insecure college student to the founder of SHI International. She outlines her method of decade-by-decade life planning, emphasizes the importance of holistic goal setting, and offers contrarian advice to entrepreneurs about avoiding outside funding and delaying IPOs.

principles.statement: Lack of external funding is often a blessing in disguise.
principles.rationale: Being unable to raise venture capital or bank credit forces a company to grow organically, which instills a deep operational discipline that cannot be easily replicated. It also prevents founders from losing control of their business.
principles.source_quote: because we weren't able to raise money all of our growth over the last twenty nine years had to be organic and that gives a company a certain amount of discipline that cannot easily be really covered

principles.statement: Be your own best advocate and focus only on what you can control.
principles.rationale: Insecurities about being an outsider or lacking certain skills can be paralyzing. To succeed, you must stop beating yourself up over external factors and direct all your energy toward actionable steps.
principles.source_quote: don't beat yourself up only focus on things that you have control over don't worry about it rest of it

principles.statement: It is entirely acceptable to fail.
principles.rationale: The fear of failure shouldn't stop you from pursuing ambitious goals. Even if a venture does not succeed, the attempt itself is valuable because you will know you gave it your best effort.
principles.source_quote: it is okay to fail at least you will have tried and you will have done your best

frameworks.name: Decade-by-Decade Life Planning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When crafting long-term life, career, and personal goals to ensure holistic success without burning out.
steps: Assume you will live to be 100 years old to establish a long-term horizon.
Create a template for each decade of your life.
Assign a singular, overarching focus to each decade (e.g., 20s for learning/preparation, 30s for extreme startup focus, 40s for family integration).
Score yourself regularly on whether you are accomplishing the specific goals within your current decade.
frameworks.source_quote: actually assumed first of all that I would live to a hundred and actually put together a template for each decade

frameworks.name: Holistic SMART Goals
frameworks.when_to_apply: When setting specific objectives within your life plan, particularly for health and family.
steps: Divide your life into three holistic aspects: personal/family, career/financial, and health.
Make goals Specific, Measurable, Actionable, Relatable, and Time-bound.
Ensure health goals are prioritized first, as they are the foundation for the other two aspects.
frameworks.source_quote: setting goals you need to make it smart so specific measurable actionable relatable time-bound

mental_models.name: The Biological Timetable
mental_models.description: Viewing family planning as a fixed biological constraint that must be strategically factored into long-term career sequencing, rather than left to chance.
mental_models.example: She dedicated her 30s entirely to her startup to avoid distractions, but explicitly planned to start a family at 39 because she 'knew about the fertility curve' and the set timetable for securing a family.

mental_models.name: Health as the Ultimate Prerequisite
mental_models.description: Treating physical health not as a separate goal, but as the foundational prerequisite that enables all career and financial goals to exist.
mental_models.example: Setting a specific goal to live beyond 100 by tracking BMI, weight, and cholesterol annually, reasoning that 'without your health there's really nothing else right that matters in life'.

heuristics: Only focus on things that you have control over.
Delay going public as long as possible.
Begin with the end in mind.

signature_quotes.text: I decided that with my strengths and weaknesses... that I could only be truly successful if I were to own and run my own company.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining her realization at age 17 that entrepreneurship was her best path forward.

signature_quotes.text: Don't beat yourself up only focus on things that you have control over don't worry about it rest of it.
signature_quotes.context: Advice on overcoming insecurity and being your own best advocate.

signature_quotes.text: If you don't [raise money] it's okay because maybe like myself you would be grateful for not being able to raise money 30 years later.
signature_quotes.context: Encouraging entrepreneurs who are struggling to find external investors.

signature_quotes.text: Without your health there's really nothing else right that matters in life.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why health goals are the foundation of her holistic life plan.

signature_quotes.text: It is okay to fail at least you will have tried and you will have done your best.
signature_quotes.context: Closing advice to entrepreneurs embarking on scary journeys.

anti_patterns.description: Rushing to take a company public (IPO).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: There is a 'whole lot of negative that comes from running a public company.' It is better to grow organically and delay going public as long as possible.

anti_patterns.description: Taking outside investment (VC or private equity) too early or unnecessarily.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: If you take outside investors, 'pretty soon you'll find that you will lose control over not only the company but but many other aspects of your business.'
